title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee approved several contracts and discussed ongoing infrastructure projects. Key actions included awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, approving a $94,860 intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction, and directing staff to develop $100/day fine language for illegal sewer discharges. The committee also learned the village received a local road improvement grant covering over 50% of a $1.5 million Birch Street reconstruction project.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
2025 Water Utility PSC annual report review
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue settlement repairs by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street repairs by Melvin. A Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting was tentatively scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM.

Michael presented the 2025 annual report showing net operating income of $482,911, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility's total value reached nearly $34 million, a 6.7% increase from infrastructure placed in service including Wells 7 and 8 and water main from the Weston Avenue project. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. The committee voted to acknowledge the report, passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term state transportation funding solutions. The village has decreased its public works budget recently and relies on grant funding for major projects while needing recurring annual funding for maintenance and snow plowing. Wisconsin is noted as affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at the Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. The competing bid was $4.25/ton. Michael demonstrated this program saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, equating to roughly $130,000 savings across this year's three projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Staff identified residents illegally connecting sump pumps to sanitary sewers. The committee discussed enforcement options under existing code allowing fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Trustee Zogami suggested waiting for the INI study results to identify problem areas, then sending violation letters with 30-60 day correction periods. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day passed unanimously.

Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1, which currently lacks water/sewer main frontage. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkkey Avenue is reconstructed, at which time the owner would pay assessment costs. Committee requested adding a specific date; Michael agreed to add 'no later than 2029.' Motion to recommend approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road reconstruction. Weston's share for water main relocation and sanitary sewer work totals an estimated $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Services for Alderson/Jelinek and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael received proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelinek intersection (roundabout), MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 and Clark Dietz $73,730; staff recommended Clark Dietz due to having experienced full-time staff rather than summer interns. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900
Adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding solutions
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines not exceeding $100/day
Enter agreement with 2230 Radkkey Avenue property owner for temporary water/sewer connection with assessment due no later than 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction at estimated $94,860
Award Alderson/Jelinek construction services to MSA and Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs Wednesday/Thursday this week

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was cancelled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals and educational presentations
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: The commission elected officers for the reorganized committee. Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously with no opposition.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. The motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported last year's attendance was estimated at 11,000-12,000 people and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was cancelled due to permit issues, making their event the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer hotel rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel usage for future reports. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with the motion passing unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting the winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese and expressed gratitude for the community's hospitality. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and established a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. The commission also approved minutes and routine staff reports.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police and court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline easement running under the parking lot area. The 36-inch deep gas line allows parking over it but required reorienting the building to higher ground, reducing fill costs. Discussion addressed future expansion capability to the north, the alternate storage shed placement, and landscaping meeting current relaxed code. Motion to approve with conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg Road that never went through the wellhead protection conditional use process and is closest to the village well. The commission agreed to reach out to that facility. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion included potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He urged the commission to use common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous Green Heck Turner building standards, to avoid unnecessary taxpayer expense. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt storage shed inspection program to track compliance visits.
actionItems: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan approved with conditions 2-5, proceeding to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation in wellhead protection district with reporting to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg Road that lacks conditional use approval
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invite for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No votes on substantive policy items were taken; the only action was approval of the April 1st meeting minutes.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg attending remotely)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations and functions required by statute - no items for this meeting
Educational presentations introduction and committee structure overview
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Lance Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting aye.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect against environmental hazards. She highlighted their 1,200+ licenses for restaurants, pools, and hotels; communicable disease prevention including recent tick kit distribution through free public health vending machines; the Nurse Family Partnership program with its first two-year-old graduates; and a new FTE position for homeless system response funded through the county board and city of Wausau partnership. The department has about 37.6 FTE across environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administrative teams.

Administrator Lance Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. He explained the department's divisions including child welfare, child protective services, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. With nearly 140 FTE, it's the county's second largest department. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability among child welfare families, noting significant numbers of participants had never had credit accounts, bank accounts, or formal leases before the program.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported the department now has 3.0 FTE as of 2025, increased from 2.75. Customer interactions jumped from 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. Veterans in Marathon County received $44 million annually in benefits, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. He discussed concerns about 2025 Marathon County suicides (30 total, 7 veterans) and efforts toward applying for the Staff Sergeant Gordon Fox VA suicide prevention grant. The office is working with Center for Veterans Issues on housing stability and will meet with Aspirus Hospice about proper death certificate documentation for surviving spouse benefits.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties as an integrated organization providing resource services and Older Americans Act aging services. He described significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The Older Americans Act hasn't been reauthorized in over two years. The organization has 60 FTEs (about 75 staff) and relies on 385-400 volunteers. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. They had their first levy increase request in 2024 after remaining flat since formation in 2011.

Chair Boots noted that a task force formed last session (with Supervisors Cavelli and Conway participating) identified community needs for the homeless population, resulting in the new FTE position in the Health Department for homeless system response that Health Officer Suginaka mentioned.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare will be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested future agenda items
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan information to upcoming meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and engaged in extensive discussion about farmers market parking challenges at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and appointed two new members to the Community Liaison and Public Safety (CLIPS) committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public comments - Jim Pitel regarding engineering consultants and committee appointments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fee amendments
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledgment of reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Work product transmittals including building permits and budget status
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplex units, and from $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb supported the fee increase noting population growth requires more park infrastructure. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously, as was Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional reciprocal inspection program with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and Safer. A food truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker valid across all jurisdictions, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote attendance policy. Discussion centered on removing language requiring members to justify absences with health, safety, or welfare reasons. Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy by striking sentences requiring justification for remote attendance. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Herling and Lopes-Serrell. The original motion as amended then passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Herling, second by Lopes-Serrell, approved unanimously. The board also discussed having committee application resumes included in future board packets for transparency.

Extensive discussion occurred regarding parking challenges at Kennedy Park's new farmers market location after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue site to development. Trustee Beck expressed safety concerns about vehicles parking on grass areas. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the original location to 53 at Kennedy Park, and vendors appreciate being able to keep vehicles with them. The board discussed potential alternatives including Mock Miller Park but noted it may be too remote. No action was taken; discussion only.

Jim Pitel criticized spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. Public Works Director Michael explained the position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skill set is difficult to find in central Wisconsin, noting even consulting firms are pulling staff from southeastern Wisconsin offices.

Michael reported that the DOT engineering study classified Weston Avenue as a 35 mph road, but traffic data showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph. A second speed study is needed to justify changing the posted limit. A trustee noted the current posted speed is inconsistent with village ordinance for over two months since the board directed a change.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 2nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment, and complained about lack of detailed Kennedy Park project information in meeting packets. He also commented during the park impact fee public hearing questioning whether the village has adequate data to justify fee amounts. No other public comments were offered; several individuals were online but none spoke.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Ordinance 26-010 approved increasing park and recreation impact fees
Resolution 2026-015 approved amending village fee schedule for park impact fees
Ordinance 26-008 approved establishing regional mobile food vendor inspection reciprocity
Ordinance 26-009 approved amending standing committees structure
Ordinance 26-011 approved amending nuisance regulations
Ordinance 26-012 approved amending bicycle and e-bike regulations
Resolution 2026-014 approved supporting sustainable transportation funding
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1 at 6:00 PM
Staff to prepare intergovernmental agreement to assist Town of Ringle with building inspections for tornado-damaged homes
Committee application resumes to be included in future board packets
Speed study for Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to board

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees from $65 to approximately $36.94 per ton for debris from the recent severe weather emergency affecting Ringle and other areas. The board suspended rules to consider the emergency tipping fee resolution after hearing from affected residents.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Committee appointments presentation by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large executive committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and respecting supervisor preferences. Gibbs noted that over 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large executive committee positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for children and elders. After paper ballot voting, Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that devastated Ringle and other areas. The board suspended rules to consider the resolution that hadn't gone through a standing committee. The state waived $13.10, Ringle waived $2.33, and the county reduced its portion to bring fees from $65 to approximately $36.94 per ton. Residents Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely. Leonard mentioned exploring partnerships with the Community Foundation for additional relief. The resolution passed unanimously.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent agenda. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission by Supervisor Rosenberg to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Toasty requested the county waive fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle were waiving their portions and that businesses had donated $40,000+ in cleanup efforts. She questioned county presence during cleanup. Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, requesting fee relief for underinsured homeowners and extension beyond 60 days since many insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved: Landfill tipping fees reduced to approximately $36.94 per ton for disaster-related debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to explore partnership with Community Foundation for reimbursing unreimbursed insurance costs for affected landowners
Solid waste management board directed to develop a clear policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Staff to track all certified disaster-related debris for potential state disaster aid documentation

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board for tornado victims. The fee waiver was amended to only cover septic system reconnect fees ($160) rather than all zoning and septic-related fees originally proposed.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry
Department overview: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld moved and Supervisor Drebeck seconded approval of the minutes. The motion passed unanimously on voice vote.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and town approval, Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the request to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about conservancy implications; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-impacted properties in towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to individual disaster victims not in declared areas. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution; Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then amended the motion to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or septic fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed unanimously. The amended motion to forward to county board passed but was not unanimous.

Director David Hagen Busher presented on department operations, explaining the landfill accepts about 1,000 tons daily with 11 FTEs. He highlighted the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, discussed leachate treatment challenges requiring a $6 million on-site treatment facility by 2032 due to PFAS regulations, and noted lithium-ion battery fires occur about weekly.

Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented for Director Jamie who had an emergency. He noted the department has 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees serving both city and county. A Nine Mile County Forest study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million annually. Supervisor Sefeld announced Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years, with Joe Tucker selected as successor.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - waiving only septic reconnect fees ($160) for April 17, 2026 tornado victims
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning approval to county board
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning approval to county board
Staff to track costs and requests related to fee waivers for future policy development
Solid Waste to resubmit environmental loan application for leachate treatment facility by October 2025 for 2027 funding

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved kayak launch signage for three village locations, deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month when the committee chair can be present, and discussed maintenance challenges for graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and criticized the Friends of Kennedy Park fundraising transparency.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks Disc Golf Course Update
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail Discussion

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded with no discussion. Motion carried unanimously by voice vote.

Director Sean Oster presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from its inception through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked about the relationship between village capital funds and Friends of Kennedy Park fundraising. Sean explained the village had planned approximately $7 million in infrastructure improvements but put those plans on hold pending the master plan project. The Friends group is currently fundraising for $117,000 for lights on the upper field and has approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation. Sean noted they plan a public check presentation at their first tournament in June. The presiding officer emphasized that the Friends group is a volunteer organization that deserves grace and shouldn't be pressured to reveal numbers before their planned announcement.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks Disc Golf Course Update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the location of trees removed from the river and the wood mat installation over gas pipelines. Dan showed images of the mats being placed over the TC Energy pipelines to protect them during equipment access. The committee viewed photos of the construction process including material placement over the pipeline crossing areas.

The item was deferred to next month because Committee Chair Katrina, who requested the item, had an unforeseen issue preventing her attendance. The presiding officer stated they wanted to give her the opportunity to speak on the matter. Resident Andy's objection to the deferral was noted, and he indicated he may return next month to speak against allowing dogs at the pool.

The committee discussed challenges with removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches along the trail. Sean explained that their best removal tool is a sandblaster through a pressure washer, but equipment access is severely limited due to bridge weight restrictions and terrain. Committee members suggested anti-vandalism coatings and community volunteer cleanup days. Scott suggested people who love the trail would volunteer to help with maintenance. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options and potentially organize volunteer efforts.

Dan Kunst from Marathon County presented version 7 of proposed kayak launch signage. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks landings within Weston, plus locations in Schofield. The signs feature maps, QR codes linking to river gauge information, and historical information unique to each location. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Funding comes from Marathon County Parks Foundation and state grants at no cost to the village. Motion to approve installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Sean reported that aquatic center pool repairs were completed the previous Tuesday, the pool is being filled and should be full by end of day tomorrow, dugout painting at Kennedy Park was completed, and opening day is June 6th. He clarified that his earlier report incorrectly stated $10,000 from Marathon County Parks when it was actually $15,000, which was corrected in the meeting minutes. He also announced the Mock Miller master plan process is beginning with a kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for the following Tuesday, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7pm.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. A resident from 6504 [street name inaudible] spoke in person against allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (appearing online under the name 'Vern M') from 5002 [street] Weston criticized the 4:00 PM meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 figure for baseball equipment 'too coincidental' and demanding to know where grant money and glow event funds are, stating the numbers are 'extremely convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Dog Day at Aquatic Center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate anti-vandalism coatings and cleaning options for Oaklair River Trail amenities
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks locations
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on clerk operations, elections, licensing, and departmental challenges including staffing reductions from four full-time employees to two and a half.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and operations
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April budget status report and TIF reports
discussions.body: Jessica provided the April budget status report and TIF one and TIF two detail reports. Regarding TIF closure, she explained bonds are due October 1st and the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. A committee member confirmed the final numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt. Motion to acknowledge items five, six, and seven was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper, passing unanimously.

discussions.item: Municipal Clerk's budget and operations presentation
discussions.body: Municipal Clerk Pam presented an overview of clerk operations including elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to currently two full-time and one part-time employee. She explained postage increased from $6,000 to $15,000 due to fall presidential elections requiring more absentee ballot mailings. Regarding pet licensing fees, she explained the increase from $10 to $20 was driven by Marathon County raising fees to cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society. Discussion included the cost of the quarterly newsletter 'The Wire' at approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing to 5,089 postal patrons.

Committee discussed costs of the Mountain Bay Metro non-dog stray contract, noting that eight mice collected last year cost approximately $175 per mouse. A committee member suggested looking at requiring law enforcement to bring in strays rather than allowing residents to do so directly, similar to other municipalities' contracts.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well. She noted Saturdays have had good attendance with full vendor capacity including food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and plant sellers. Tuesdays have been challenging due to cool and windy weather. Discussion addressed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying parking is now allowed on the west side of Alderson while east side remains no parking.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving.

actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution to call TIF bonds expected this summer for October 1st bond payment
Chief election inspector pay increase to $15/hour to be proposed for next two-year term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee spots including Evan's vacancy
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with added emoto restrictions, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both items for further research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection ordinance

discussions.item: Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, and Police Department Reports
discussions.body: Staff reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will come off the code enforcement roll after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th starting around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for all three municipalities. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 made by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet) over streets and walkways that were switched in text as discussed at the previous meeting. Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred about emotos (electric dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety hazards. The committee debated electric scooters and whether to exempt children's play scooters, ultimately deciding to leave enforcement discretion to officers. Olsen made a motion to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and any public property in the Village of Weston. Seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 4-5 hour windows. DPW Director Michael Wodalski noted they rarely plow before 4-5am and suggested eliminating the overnight parking restriction entirely, instead signing only the 6-8 problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear cars before plows arrive. The committee decided to defer the item until July, with Wodalski tasked to identify specific exception areas.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school parking lot. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000, while a lighted system would cost $25-30,000. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for crosswalks when requested by private entities. Olsen made a motion that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the cost of installation, the committee recommends board approval. Seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously. DPW Director Wodalski will contact Mount Olive about cost-sharing.

discussions.item: Mobile Food Vendors Fire Inspection Ordinance
discussions.body: Fire Chief explained a regional partnership between Weston, Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and Safer fire departments for food truck inspections. One department's inspection with a sticker is valid for all participating municipalities. Wausau opted not to participate. Fire Inspector Marty already inspected one truck this week which failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Chwanka moved to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), 3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence with secondary unit), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt added another issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski where an 8-plex has eight separate addresses plus one for utility room. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure that has been posted since 2021 and abandoned since 2010-2014. The fire department previously declined to conduct a training burn due to unsafe conditions. The current owners, who operate a small farm and had housed chickens in the structure, prefer this approach. Demolition costs will be added to the tax roll. Olsen moved to recommend authorization, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting the grass would be destroyed and noting that parking vehicles on grass appears to violate village code. He questioned whether ADA-compliant parking on grass meets requirements for firm, level surfaces. He questioned the origin of the new food truck fire inspection requirement. He also raised concerns about ambiguous language regarding when mobile food vendors are prohibited and noted that some code violations on the enforcement list have been pending for years without visible progress.
actionItems: Amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances recommended to board of trustees for approval
E-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property recommended to board of trustees
DPW Director Wodalski to identify specific problem areas for overnight parking signage and bring back to July meeting
DPW Director Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church about paying for crosswalk installation costs
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections recommended for approval
Address corrections for 5915/5917 Delicoski Street, 3913/3917 Ross Avenue, and 2211 Radkey Avenue recommended to board
Raze order proceedings authorized for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster in yard
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 p.m.

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
